name: Viking Raids and Siege of Paris (845–911)
detail: Viking longships raided the Seine valley beginning in 845, when Ragnar Lothbrok sacked Paris and received 7,000 pounds of silver to leave. Further raids climaxed in the Siege of Paris (885–886). The Carolingian kings ultimately ceded Normandy to the Viking chieftain Rollo (Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte, 911), creating the Duchy of Normandy — whose descendants would conquer England in 1066.

name: Umayyad Muslim Invasion (719–732)
detail: Umayyad forces crossed the Pyrenees from the Iberian Peninsula, capturing Narbonne (719) and advancing through Aquitaine to attack Autun (721) and Tours (732). Charles Martel's victory at the Battle of Tours (732) stopped the advance. Narbonne remained in Arab hands until 759. The invasion shaped Frankish military organization and the political alliance between the Carolingians and the papacy.

name: English Occupation of Northern France (1415–1453)
detail: Following the catastrophic defeat at Agincourt (1415) and the Treaty of Troyes (1420), Henry V of England was recognized as heir to the French throne. English forces occupied Paris and northern France for over 30 years. The Burgundian alliance with England was decisive; Joan of Arc's campaigns (1429–1430) initiated the reversal that culminated in French reconquest by 1453.

name: Allied Invasions During War of Spanish Succession (1702–1714)
detail: The Grand Alliance (Britain, Dutch Republic, Austria, Prussia) under Marlborough and Eugene of Savoy invaded France repeatedly after the spectacular defeats at Blenheim (1704) and Ramillies (1706). Allied forces reached 50 miles from Paris in 1709 after Malplaquet. France was saved by the devastation of Malplaquet (which cost the Alliance 25,000 casualties) and British political change, securing a favorable peace at Utrecht (1713).

name: Coalition Invasions of Revolutionary France (1792–1797)
detail: Austria and Prussia invaded France in August 1792 to restore Louis XVI, triggering the French Republic's mobilization of the levée en masse (universal military conscription). French victory at Valmy (September 20, 1792) stopped the initial invasion. Coalition invasions continued through 1797 as France repulsed multiple incursions and expanded into the Low Countries, Italy, and the Rhineland.

name: Allied Occupation of France (1815–1818)
detail: Following Napoleon's final defeat at Waterloo (June 1815), 1.2 million Allied troops occupied France under the terms of the Second Treaty of Paris. France paid a 700 million franc indemnity and hosted Allied garrisons in northern France until 1818. The occupation was supervised by Arthur Wellesley (Wellington) as Allied commander.

name: Prussian Siege of Paris (1870–1871)
detail: Following the defeat at Sedan (September 2, 1870) and capture of Napoleon III, Prussian armies surrounded Paris beginning September 19. The city held out for 132 days under severe famine conditions — Parisians ate rats, zoo animals, and horses — before capitulating on January 28, 1871. The resulting Treaty of Frankfurt forced France to cede Alsace-Lorraine and pay 5 billion gold francs.

name: German Invasion and Occupation — World War I (1914–1918)
detail: Germany's Schlieffen Plan invaded France through Belgium in August 1914, rapidly capturing a large portion of northeastern France. German forces reached within 30 miles of Paris before the Battle of the Marne (September 5–12, 1914) halted the advance. The industrial heartland of northeastern France (10 departments) remained under German occupation for the duration of the war, suffering systematic economic exploitation.

name: Nazi German Occupation of France (1940–1944)
detail: Following the Fall of France (June 1940), Germany occupied the northern and western zones of France (3/5 of French territory), with Vichy France as a nominally independent southern zone until November 1942, when Germany occupied the entire country. French Jews were deported to Nazi death camps (75,721 deported, fewer than 3% survived). France's industrial capacity was systematically exploited for German war production.

name: ISIS Jihadist Attack Wave (2015–2016)
detail: France suffered the deadliest terrorist attacks in Europe since the 2004 Madrid bombings: Charlie Hebdo and Hyper Cacher supermarket (January 7–9, 2015, 17 killed); Paris attacks including Bataclan (November 13, 2015, 130 killed); Nice truck attack on Bastille Day (July 14, 2016, 86 killed). The Islamic State claimed responsibility for the November 2015 and Nice attacks, with operatives trained in Syria.
